summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated attacker can obtain a superuser JWT via the auto_login endpoint and then execute arbitrary Python code on the server through the validate/code endpoint's exec() sink, achieving full remote code execution.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the crafted validate/code payload to write an attacker-controlled proof string to /tmp/evohunt_proof.txt inside the Langflow container, demonstrating that arbitrary code execution occurred without any prior authentication.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the Docker image, started the Langflow server, exploited the auto_login endpoint to obtain a superuser JWT, sent a crafted function definition to /api/v1/validate/code that wrote the proof string via default-argument evaluation, and verified the proof file inside the container.
verification_method: The exploit script writes a unique proof string (EVPHUNT_POC_PROOF_OF_RCE_2026) to /tmp/evohunt_proof.txt via the exec() sink. The verify.sh helper then checks that the file exists and contains exactly the expected proof string. This distinguishes real code execution from a startup failure, parser error, or unrelated crash.
evidence_produced_by_run: The auto_login endpoint returned a valid superuser JWT without any authentication.
The validate/code endpoint accepted the crafted function definition and exec()'d it, triggering the default-argument side effect.
The proof file /tmp/evohunt_proof.txt was created inside the container with the exact expected content.
The verification helper confirmed the proof string matched, ruling out false positives from startup errors.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository from GitHub, checks out the pinned version tag, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo for reference.
